name: Oliver Nelson
description: American jazz saxophonist, clarinetist, arranger, composer, and bandleader (1932-1975)
abstract: Oliver Edward Nelson (June 4, 1932 – October 28, 1975) was an American jazz saxophonist, clarinetist, arranger, composer, and bandleader. His 1961 Impulse!

concerts.label: WLIB Festival of Music and Drama
WLIB: 12th Annual Festival of Music and Drama

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Oliver Nelson was a big man in my band... So we hired him. Not only was he a great saxophone player, but he wrote some great arrangements for me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Etta Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Etta has collaborated with such greats as J.C. Heard, Oliver Nelson, Earl “Fatha” Hines...
about_quotes.doc_title: Etta Jones interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 2, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: I was called to play on maybe about four or five of them [films]... As a result of meeting him here and playing on those recordings... he invited me to play on this project with Count Basie.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: But one of the most, I would say, significant people in the band, for more than one reason, was a young man who was 18 years old at that particular time. His name was Oliver Nelson.

about_quotes.speaker: Roger Kellaway
about_quotes.evidence: those are the years I was doing things with Oliver Nelson. Some of the best recordings I ever made I thought... 'More Blues and the Abstract Truth' with Ollie.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roger Kellaway interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I really didn’t get to know him until 1969 when I was a part of the recording session that Oliver Nelson did with Louis Armstrong.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
